strategy_research: {"strategies": [{"name": "Keller's VAA (Vigilant Asset Allocation)", "description": "Breadth momentum strategy using a weighted 13612W score (12\u00d71m + 4\u00d73m + 2\u00d76m + 1\u00d712m returns). If ANY offensive asset scores negative, 100% shifts to best defensive asset. Overweights recent momentum to catch market turns early.", "evidence": ["Keller & Keuning (2017) 'Breadth Momentum and Vigilant Asset Allocation' \u2014 SSRN #3002624", "Follow-on DAA paper (2018) SSRN #3212862 \u2014 refined canary universe reduces defensive allocation", "AllocateSmartly live tracking confirms strategy mechanics and post-publication performance"], "historical_sharpe": "0.94\u20131.10 in-sample (1970\u20132016); ~0.50\u20130.60 out-of-sample post-2017 due to bull market drag and 2022 bond losses", "market_conditions": "Works: sharp multi-asset corrections (2000\u201302, 2008\u201309). Fails: prolonged bull markets (high opportunity cost), rising rate environments (bond-heavy defensive universe hurt in 2022), whipsaw markets with rapid V-shaped recoveries", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG", "TLT"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Antonacci's Dual Momentum (GEM)", "description": "Two sequential monthly rules: (1) Absolute momentum \u2014 did SPY beat T-Bills over 12 months? If no, hold AGG. (2) Relative momentum \u2014 did SPY beat EFA over 12 months? If yes hold SPY, else hold EFA. Single ETF held at all times.", "evidence": ["Antonacci (2012) 'Risk Premia Harvesting Through Dual Momentum' \u2014 optimalmomentum.com", "Antonacci (2014) 'Dual Momentum Investing' book", "In-sample 1974\u20132013: 17.43% CAGR vs 8.85% ACWI, max drawdown 22.7% vs 60.2%"], "historical_sharpe": "0.87 in-sample (1974\u20132013); degraded to ~0.30 out-of-sample (2014\u20132021) due to persistent US equity dominance eliminating relative momentum value", "market_conditions": "Works: market cycles with meaningful US/international rotation, bear markets (absolute momentum filter exits to AGG). Fails: prolonged US equity dominance, V-shaped recoveries (misses rally after defensive move), rising rates (AGG not riskless)", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Cross-Asset Momentum + Trend Filter + Volatility Targeting", "description": "Three stacked layers: (1) Rank ETFs by risk-adjusted momentum (12m return / realized vol). (2) Trend filter \u2014 only hold assets above 200-day SMA. (3) Volatility targeting \u2014 scale each position by target_vol / realized_vol_21d (e.g. 10% annual target). Hold top-N assets or cash when all fail trend filter.", "evidence": ["Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012) 'Time-Series Momentum' \u2014 Journal of Financial Economics: Sharpe ~0.95 across 58 futures", "Moreira & Muir (2017) 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios' \u2014 Journal of Finance: vol targeting adds +0.08\u20130.11 Sharpe", "Liu et al. (2020) GRJMOM \u2014 Lancaster University: risk-adjusted ranking improves Sharpe from 0.67 to 1.18", "Cederburg et al. (2020) \u2014 Financial Analysts Journal: conditional vol targeting consistently enhances Sharpe"], "historical_sharpe": "0.90\u20131.30 combined (individual components: time-series momentum ~0.80\u20131.0, vol targeting adds ~0.10, risk-adjusted ranking further +0.30\u20130.50)", "market_conditions": "Works: trending markets across any asset class, volatility clustering regimes. Fails: choppy directionless markets (whipsaw from trend filter), momentum crashes (Jan 2001, Mar 2009, May 2020), high-correlation environments where all assets move together", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD", "EFA"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Risk Parity with Volatility Targeting", "description": "Each asset sized so volatility contribution to portfolio is equal (weight_i \u221d 1/vol_i, or full ERC solving w_i \u00d7 (Cov\u00d7w)_i = constant). Portfolio then scaled to hit target annualized volatility of 8\u201312%. Bridges equities, long bonds, and gold whose return streams are historically anti-correlated.", "evidence": ["Harvey, Hoyle et al. (2018) 'The Impact of Volatility Targeting' \u2014 Journal of Portfolio Management (Duke PDF)", "Moreira & Muir (2017) \u2014 Journal of Finance: vol targeting adds significant Sharpe to equity and credit portfolios", "Dynamic Risk Parity \u2014 MDPI Journal of Risk and Financial Management (2026): dynamic RP Sharpe 1.418 vs static 1.368", "AQR Capital 'Understanding Risk Parity' white paper"], "historical_sharpe": "0.80\u20131.42 depending on implementation (static inverse-vol ~0.80, dynamic ERC with vol targeting up to 1.42 per MDPI 2026)", "market_conditions": "Works: deflationary recessions (stocks fall, bonds rise), negative stock-bond correlation, long time horizons. Fails critically: inflationary regimes \u2014 2022 saw All Weather lose 22%, worse than 60/40 at -16%, as stocks and bonds fell simultaneously", "instruments": ["SPY", "VTI", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Regime-Based Rotation (VIX + Yield Curve)", "description": "Composite macro regime signal combining: (1) VIX level/term structure \u2014 contango=risk-on, backwardation=defensive; (2) 10yr-2yr or 10yr-3m yield spread \u2014 inversion triggers shift toward TLT/AGG. Equity allocation scales continuously with regime composite rather than binary switches.", "evidence": ["SOA Research (2025): VIX+MOVE ratio model backtested 14.03% CAGR 2014\u20132025, down only in 2018 and 2022", "Estrella & Mishkin (1998) 'Predicting US Recessions' \u2014 Review of Economics and Statistics: yield curve as leading indicator", "Nature Scientific Reports (2025): ML LSTM regime-switch model Sharpe 1.38 out-of-sample (2017\u20132022), 55% improvement over static RP", "Wright (2006) FEDS paper on yield curve recession probability"], "historical_sharpe": "0.70\u20131.10 (VIX+MOVE model), 1.38 for ML-enhanced version out-of-sample 2017\u20132022", "market_conditions": "Works: fear-driven corrections with VIX leading (VIX spikes weeks before full equity selloff), economic turning points where yield curve inverts 6\u201318 months ahead. Fails: flash crashes/geopolitical shocks (VIX spikes and reverses before strategy acts), inflationary bear markets where bonds fail as safe harbor", "instruments": ["SPY", "VTI", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP)", "description": "Lopez de Prado's graph-theoretic portfolio construction: (1) Build correlation-based distance matrix and hierarchical dendrogram clustering similar assets. (2) Quasi-diagonalize covariance matrix. (3) Recursively bisect tree, allocating capital proportional to inverse variance at each branch. Avoids covariance matrix inversion \u2014 more stable than Markowitz with limited history.", "evidence": ["L\u00f3pez de Prado (2016) 'Building Diversified Portfolios that Outperform Out-of-Sample' \u2014 Journal of Portfolio Management Vol.42 No.4, SSRN #2708678", "Out-of-sample: 31.3% Sharpe improvement over CLA (Markowitz), 72% lower variance vs CLA, 38% lower variance vs inverse-vol parity", "Efficient HRP Implementation (2025) \u2014 ScienceDirect: confirms robustness improvements", "HRP Latin American markets (2025) \u2014 arXiv: cross-market validation"], "historical_sharpe": "~31% Sharpe improvement over classical Markowitz out-of-sample; estimated absolute Sharpe 0.80\u20131.00 on multi-asset ETF universe; net Sharpe ~0.94 after accounting for higher turnover vs ERC", "market_conditions": "Works: unstable correlations (HRP adapts to shifting structure), noisy covariance estimation with small samples or many assets. Fails: persistent bull markets (defensive structure limits upside), high turnover can erode net returns vs simpler methods", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD", "EFA"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "high"}, {"name": "Stacked Signal Ensemble (Multi-Factor Composite)", "description": "Combines 4\u20136 orthogonal signals across regimes: 12m momentum (trend), 1m short-term reversal, low-volatility tilt, carry (yield spread), macro breadth score, and credit spread level. Each signal z-scored and combined via equal-weight or regression. Position sizing uses composite_signal_strength \u00d7 inverse_vol.", "evidence": ["Goldman Sachs AM (2018) 'How to Combine Investment Signals in Long/Short Strategies': integration approach achieves higher information ratios than signal mixing", "Macrosynergy Research (2024): optimized macro signals achieve Sharpe 0.8\u20131.2 vs 0.6 for raw signals", "Liu et al. GRJMOM (2020): risk-adjusted momentum alone Sharpe 1.18", "ML ensemble TAA practitioner backtests (2021\u20132025): Sharpe 1.34\u20131.56"], "historical_sharpe": "1.18\u20131.56 (backtested, heavily overstated due to in-sample optimization); estimated out-of-sample 0.70\u20131.10 with careful signal selection and validation", "market_conditions": "Works: any regime in theory \u2014 different signals activate in different environments, ensemble averages regime risk. Fails: universal sell-offs where correlations spike to 1 and all signals fail simultaneously; high overfitting risk makes OOS degradation severe", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD", "EFA"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "high"}, {"name": "Cross-Asset Lead-Lag (Credit Spread Signal)", "description": "Uses high-yield credit spread widening as a forward indicator for equity weakness. Mechanism: credit markets are dominated by informed institutional investors who price distress risk faster than equity retail. Monitor HYG price momentum or HYG/LQD spread as proxy for CDX HY index. Widen threshold triggers equity exposure reduction and shift toward TLT/GLD.", "evidence": ["Gilchrist & Zakraj\u0161ek (2012) 'Credit Spreads and Business Cycle Fluctuations' \u2014 American Economic Review / NBER WP #17021: excess bond premium explains 30\u201340% of future equity return variance at 1\u20134 quarter horizons", "Faust, Gilchrist, Wright & Zakraj\u0161ek (2012) NBER WP #16725: credit spreads significantly improve economic forecasting in recessions", "Journal of International Financial Markets (2014): CDS \u2192 equity lead confirmed for non-investment-grade firms", "Journal of Financial Economics (2025): bond\u2192equity lead-lag confirmed as tradeable without lookahead bias"], "historical_sharpe": "Not directly quantified as standalone; component studies show statistically significant alpha: EBP explains 30\u201340% of equity variance (Gilchrist & Zakraj\u0161ek), CDS\u2192equity lead particularly strong in crisis periods (RAPS 2021). Best used as overlay \u2014 estimated Sharpe contribution 0.10\u20130.25 added to base strategy", "market_conditions": "Works: pre-recession credit stress (2007\u201308, 2015\u201316, 2018, 2020 \u2014 credit widened days/weeks before equity peak). Fails: equity-led shocks (geopolitical, flash crashes) where credit lags equity, investment-grade credit (signal strongest in HY), low-spread compression regimes", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "high"}], "recommendation": "Start with Cross-Asset Momentum + Trend Filter + Volatility Targeting (Strategy 3). It has the deepest multi-paper academic validation (Moskowitz 2012, Moreira & Muir 2017, Cederburg 2020), a clean economic mechanism (momentum + leverage effect), and a realistic out-of-sample Sharpe of 0.90\u20131.10 \u2014 meaningfully above SPY buy-and-hold (~0.55). It works with all 8 available instruments, requires only monthly or weekly rebalancing, and scales naturally to small capital ($1000\u2013$5000) with no leverage required. The volatility targeting layer directly addresses the moderate risk tolerance constraint by dynamically reducing position sizes during high-volatility periods. Implementation is Python-manageable: rank ETFs by (12m_return / realized_vol_21d), filter to those above 200-day SMA, hold top 3 assets, scale each position so annualized vol contribution = 10% target. VAA (Strategy 1) is the simplest alternative for a lower-complexity first attempt, but its post-2017 live performance has been disappointing. Use Credit Spread Lead-Lag (Strategy 8) as a risk-off overlay once the core strategy is running."}
